questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water is boiled in a container whose bottom has a surface area of $25 \text{ cm}^2$, a thickness of $1.0 \text{ mm}$, and a thermal conductivity of $50 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. After boiling begins, $100 \text{ g}$ of water is converted into steam per minute during the steady state. Assuming no heat loss to the atmosphere, determine the temperature of the lower surface of the bottom. The latent heat of vaporization of water is $2.26 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat transfer through the bottom of the container is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(T_2 - T_1)}{d}$

The rate of heat utilized in boiling the water is:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{mL}{t}$

Equating the two rates of heat transfer:

$\dfrac{KA(T_2 - T_1)}{d} = \dfrac{mL}{t}$

Substituting the given values, $K = 50 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, $A = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $d = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $m = 0.1 \text{ kg}$, $t = 60 \text{ s}$, $L = 2.26 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, and $T_1 = 100 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$\dfrac{50 \times 25 \times 10^{-4} \times (T_2 - 100)}{10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 2.26 \times 10^6}{60}$

$125(T_2 - 100) = \dfrac{226000}{60}$

$125(T_2 - 100) = \dfrac{11300}{3}$

$T_2 - 100 = \dfrac{11300}{375} \approx 30.13 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$

$T_2 = 100 + 30.13 = 130.13 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$

The temperature of the lower surface is approximately $130 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel rod ($K = 46 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$) of length $1.0 \text{ m}$ has one end kept in ice at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and the opposite end in boiling water at $100^\circ\text{C}$. The rod has a cross-sectional area of $0.04 \text{ cm}^2$. Assuming there is no heat loss to the surrounding atmosphere, determine the mass of ice melting per second. The latent heat of fusion of ice is $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat transfer through the rod is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$H = \dfrac{KA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$

Substituting the given values:
$K = 46 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$A = 0.04 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
$T_1 - T_2 = 100^\circ\text{C} - 0^\circ\text{C} = 100^\circ\text{C}$
$L = 1.0 \text{ m}$

$H = \dfrac{46 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{1.0} = 1.84 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J s}^{-1}$

The heat transferred per second is used to melt the ice. The mass of ice melting per second $m$ is given by:

$m = \dfrac{H}{L_f}$

Given the latent heat of fusion of ice $L_f = 3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$:

$m = \dfrac{1.84 \times 10^{-2}}{3.36 \times 10^5} \text{ kg s}^{-1}$

$m \approx 5.476 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg s}^{-1}$

Converting the mass to grams per second:

$m = 5.476 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^3 \text{ g s}^{-1} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person's normal body-temperature is $97 \text{ }^\circ F$. Determine the rate at which heat flows out of their body through the clothes, assuming the following values: room temperature $= 47 \text{ }^\circ F$, surface of the body under clothes $= 1.6 \text{ m}^2$, conductivity of the cloth $= 0.04 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, and thickness of the cloth $= 0.5 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
The temperature difference in Fahrenheit is $\Delta T_{F} = 97 - 47 = 50 \text{ }^\circ F$

Converting this temperature difference to Celsius:

$\Delta T_{C} = \dfrac{5}{9} \times \Delta T_{F} = \dfrac{5}{9} \times 50 = \dfrac{250}{9} \text{ }^\circ C$

The rate of heat flow is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA \Delta T}{d}$

Substituting the given values $K = 0.04 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, $A = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$, and $d = 0.5 \text{ cm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 1.6 \times \dfrac{250}{9}}{0.005}$

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = 8 \times 1.6 \times \dfrac{250}{9} = \dfrac{3200}{9} \approx 355.56 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the rate of heat flow is $356 \text{ J s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pitcher having $1\text{-mm}$ thick porous walls holds $10 \text{ kg}$ of water. Water reaches the outer surface and evaporates at a rate of $0.1 \text{ g s}^{-1}$. The surface area of the pitcher (one side) is $200 \text{ cm}^2$. The room temperature is $42^\circ \text{C}$, the latent heat of vaporization is $2.27 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, and the thermal conductivity of the porous walls is $0.80 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$. Determine the temperature of the water in the pitcher once it attains a constant value.
questions.solution:
In steady state, the heat required for evaporation of water is supplied by the heat conducted from the surroundings through the walls of the pitcher.

The rate of heat required for evaporation is:
$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = L \dfrac{dm}{dt}$

Substituting the given values:
$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = (2.27 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg s}^{-1}) = 227 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

The rate of heat conduction through the walls is given by:
$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{K A (T_0 - T)}{d}$

Equating the two rates:
$\dfrac{0.80 \times (200 \times 10^{-4}) \times (42 - T)}{10^{-3}} = 227$

$\dfrac{0.016}{10^{-3}} \times (42 - T) = 227$

$16(42 - T) = 227$

$42 - T = \dfrac{227}{16} = 14.1875$

$T = 42 - 14.1875 = 27.8125^\circ \text{C}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $T \approx 28^\circ \text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform slab measuring $10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm} \times 1 \text{ cm}$ is placed between two heat reservoirs maintained at temperatures of $10^\circ \text{C}$ and $90^\circ \text{C}$. The larger surface areas of the slab are in contact with the reservoirs. If the thermal conductivity of the material is $0.80 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$, determine the amount of heat flowing through the slab per minute.
questions.solution:
Area of the larger surface, $A = 10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm} = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$

Thickness of the slab, $d = 1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

Temperature difference, $\Delta T = 90^\circ\text{C} - 10^\circ\text{C} = 80^\circ\text{C}$

Time, $t = 1 \text{ minute} = 60 \text{ s}$

Thermal conductivity, $K = 0.80 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

The amount of heat flowing through the slab is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$Q = \dfrac{K A \Delta T t}{d}$

Substituting the given values:

$Q = \dfrac{0.80 \times 10^{-2} \times 80 \times 60}{10^{-2}}$

$Q = 0.80 \times 80 \times 60$

$Q = 3840 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water at $50^\circ C$ is contained in a closed cylindrical vessel of height $10 \text{ cm}$ and cross sectional area $10 \text{ cm}^2$. The walls of the vessel are adiabatic, while the flat parts are constructed of $1$-mm thick aluminium ($K = 200 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$). Assume the outside temperature is $20^\circ C$. Given that the density of water is $1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$ and its specific heat capacity is $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, estimate the time taken for the temperature of the water to fall by $1.0^\circ C$. Make any necessary simplifying assumptions.
questions.solution:
Volume of water in the cylinder is given by:

$V = A \times h = 10 \text{ cm}^2 \times 10 \text{ cm} = 100 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$

Mass of the water is:

$m = \rho V = 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 0.1 \text{ kg}$

The heat lost by the water to cool by $\Delta T = 1.0^\circ \text{C}$ is:

$Q = mc\Delta T = 0.1 \times 4200 \times 1.0 = 420 \text{ J}$

Heat is lost only through the two flat parts (top and bottom) of the cylinder, as the walls are adiabatic. The total area for heat conduction is:

$A_{\text{total}} = 2A = 2 \times 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

Assuming the temperature difference remains approximately constant at $\Delta T_{\text{cond}} = 50^\circ \text{C} - 20^\circ \text{C} = 30^\circ \text{C}$, the rate of heat loss is:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{K A_{\text{total}} \Delta T_{\text{cond}}}{d}$

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{200 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 30}{10^{-3}} = 12000 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

The time taken for the temperature to fall by $1.0^\circ \text{C}$ is:

$t = \dfrac{Q}{\dfrac{dQ}{dt}} = \dfrac{420}{12000} = 0.035 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The left end of a copper rod (length $= 20 \text{ cm}$, area of cross section $= 0.20 \text{ cm}^2$) is kept at $20^\circ \text{C}$ while the right end is kept at $80^\circ \text{C}$. Ignoring any heat loss due to radiation, determine the temperature at a point $11 \text{ cm}$ from the left end. Thermal conductivity of copper $= 385 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
In steady state, the temperature gradient along a uniform rod is constant.

The temperature $T$ at a distance $x$ from the left end is given by

$T = T_L + \dfrac{T_R - T_L}{L} x$

Substituting the given values $T_L = 20^\circ \text{C}$, $T_R = 80^\circ \text{C}$, $L = 20 \text{ cm}$, and $x = 11 \text{ cm}$

$T = 20 + \dfrac{80 - 20}{20} \times 11$

$T = 20 + \dfrac{60}{20} \times 11$

$T = 20 + 3 \times 11$

$T = 20 + 33 = 53^\circ \text{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An icebox almost entirely filled with ice at $0^\circ C$ is submerged in a large volume of water at $20^\circ C$. The walls of the box have a surface area of $2400 \text{ cm}^2$, a thickness of $2.0 \text{ mm}$, and a thermal conductivity of $0.06 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$. Determine the rate at which the ice melts inside the box. (Latent heat of fusion of ice $= 3.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$)
questions.solution:
The rate of heat transfer through the walls of the icebox is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{kA(T_2 - T_1)}{d}$

Substituting the given values:
$k = 0.06 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$A = 2400 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.24 \text{ m}^2$
$T_2 - T_1 = 20 - 0 = 20^\circ\text{C}$
$d = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{0.06 \times 0.24 \times 20}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 144 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

The rate at which the ice melts is given by:

$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{L} \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

Substituting $L = 3.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{144}{3.4 \times 10^5} \text{ kg s}^{-1}$

To find the rate in $\text{kg h}^{-1}$, multiply by $3600 \text{ s}$:

$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{144 \times 3600}{3.4 \times 10^5} \text{ kg h}^{-1}$

$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{518400}{340000} \approx 1.52 \text{ kg h}^{-1}$

Rounding off to two significant figures, the rate at which the ice melts is $1.5 \text{ kg h}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metre stick has its ends maintained at $100^\circ C$ and $0^\circ C$. One end of a rod is kept at $25^\circ C$. At what point on the metre stick should the other end of the rod be touched so that zero heat current flows through the rod in steady state?
questions.solution:
In steady state, the temperature gradient along the metre stick is uniform.

Let the $0^\circ C$ end be at $x = 0$ and the $100^\circ C$ end be at $x = 100 \text{ cm}$.

The temperature $T$ at a distance $x$ from the $0^\circ C$ end is given by:

$T = 0 + \dfrac{100 - 0}{100} x = x$

For zero heat current to flow through the rod, the temperature at the point of contact on the metre stick must be equal to the temperature of the other end of the rod, which is $25^\circ C$.

Equating the temperatures, we get:

$x = 25$

Thus, the point of contact should be $25 \text{ cm}$ from the $0^\circ C$ end.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The left end of a copper rod (length $= 20 \text{ cm}$, area of cross section $= 0.20 \text{ cm}^2$) is kept at $20^\circ \text{C}$ while the right end is kept at $80^\circ \text{C}$. Ignoring any heat loss due to radiation, determine the heat current through the rod. Thermal conductivity of copper $= 385 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Given parameters of the copper rod:

Length, $L = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$

Area of cross section, $A = 0.20 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Temperature difference, $\Delta T = 80^\circ \text{C} - 20^\circ \text{C} = 60^\circ \text{C}$

Thermal conductivity, $k = 385 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$

The heat current $H$ through the rod is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$H = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{L}$

Substituting the given values:

$H = \dfrac{385 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-4} \times 60}{0.20}$

$H = 385 \times 10^{-4} \times 60$

$H = 23100 \times 10^{-4}$

$H = 2.31 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water is held in a container with $2.0$-mm thick walls composed of a material having a thermal conductivity of $0.50 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, as shown in the figure. The container is immersed in a melting-ice bath at $0^\circ\text{C}$. The total surface area of the walls in contact with the water is $0.05 \text{ m}^2$. A wheel is clamped inside the water and is coupled to a block of mass $M$. The wheel rotates as the block descends. After some time, a steady state is established where the block descends with a constant speed of $10 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$ and the water temperature remains steady at $1.0^\circ\text{C}$. Determine the mass $M$ of the block, assuming heat flows out of the water only through the contact walls. Use $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
At steady state, the rate of mechanical work done by the descending block is equal to the rate of heat conducted through the walls of the container.

The mechanical power generated by the block is:
$P = Mgv$

The rate of heat conduction through the container walls is given by Fourier's law:
$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{kA(T_w - T_0)}{d}$

Equating the mechanical power to the rate of heat conduction:
$Mgv = \dfrac{kA(T_w - T_0)}{d}$

Given values are:
$k = 0.50 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$
$T_w = 1.0^\circ\text{C}$
$T_0 = 0^\circ\text{C}$
$d = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$v = 10 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
$g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

Substituting the values into the equation:
$M \times 10 \times 0.1 = \dfrac{0.50 \times 0.05 \times (1.0 - 0)}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}$

$M \times 1 = \dfrac{0.025}{0.002}$

$M = 12.5 \text{ kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a winter day, the atmospheric temperature falls to $-10^\circ C$, and ice begins to form on the surface of a lake. Determine the rate of increase of the ice thickness when $10 \text{ cm}$ of ice has already formed. Assume the temperature of the entire body of water reaches $0^\circ C$ prior to the onset of ice formation. Take the density of water as $1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, the latent heat of fusion of ice as $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, and the thermal conductivity of ice as $1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$. Neglect the expansion of water upon freezing.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat conduction through the ice layer is given by:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(T_w - T_a)}{x}$

The rate of heat released due to the formation of ice of thickness $dx$ is:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \rho A L \dfrac{dx}{dt}$

Equating the two rates of heat transfer:

$\rho A L \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(T_w - T_a)}{x}$

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{K(T_w - T_a)}{\rho L x}$

Substituting the given values, $K = 1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$, $T_w = 0^\circ \text{C}$, $T_a = -10^\circ \text{C}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, $L = 3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, and $x = 0.1 \text{ m}$:

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{1.7 \times (0 - (-10))}{1000 \times 3.36 \times 10^5 \times 0.1}$

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{17}{3.36 \times 10^7}$

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} \approx 5.06 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a winter day, the atmospheric temperature falls to $-10^\circ C$, and ice begins to form on the surface of a lake. Determine the total time required for the formation of $10 \text{ cm}$ of ice. Assume the temperature of the entire body of water reaches $0^\circ C$ prior to the onset of ice formation. Take the density of water as $1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, the latent heat of fusion of ice as $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, and the thermal conductivity of ice as $1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$. Neglect the expansion of water upon freezing.
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the thickness of ice at time $t$. The rate of heat transfer through the ice layer is given by:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA \Delta T}{x}$

The heat released when a small thickness $dx$ of ice forms is:

$dQ = dm \cdot L = (\rho A dx) L$

Equating the two expressions for the rate of heat transfer:

$\rho A L \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{KA \Delta T}{x}$

$\int_{0}^{x} x dx = \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{K \Delta T}{\rho L} dt$

$\dfrac{x^2}{2} = \dfrac{K \Delta T}{\rho L} t$

$t = \dfrac{\rho L x^2}{2 K \Delta T}$

Substituting the given values ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, $L = 3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$, $x = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $K = 1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, $\Delta T = 10^\circ\text{C}$):

$t = \dfrac{1000 \times 3.36 \times 10^5 \times (0.1)^2}{2 \times 1.7 \times 10}$

$t = \dfrac{3.36 \times 10^6}{34} \text{ s}$

$t \approx 98823.5 \text{ s}$

Converting the time into hours:

$t = \dfrac{98823.5}{3600} \text{ hours} \approx 27.45 \text{ hours} \approx 27.5 \text{ hours}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a winter day, the atmospheric temperature drops to $-10^\circ \text{C}$ and ice forms on the surface of a lake. Suppose that the water temperature at the bottom of the lake stays constant at $4^\circ \text{C}$ while the ice forms (the heat required to maintain the bottom layer's temperature may be supplied by the lake bed). The lake has a depth of $1.0 \text{ m}$. Determine the steady state maximum value of the ice thickness. The thermal conductivity of water is $0.50 \text{ W m}^{-1}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, the thermal conductivity of ice is $1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, the density of water is $1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, and the latent heat of fusion of ice is $3.36 \times 10^5 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$. Neglect the expansion of water upon freezing.
questions.solution:
Let the maximum steady-state thickness of the ice be $x$. The thickness of the water layer below the ice is $1.0 - x$.

At the steady state of maximum ice thickness, the rate of heat conducted upward through the water layer equals the rate of heat conducted upward through the ice layer. The temperature at the ice-water interface is $0^\circ \text{C}$.

The rate of heat flow per unit area through the water layer is:

$\dfrac{Q_w}{A t} = \dfrac{k_w (T_b - T_i)}{1.0 - x}$

The rate of heat flow per unit area through the ice layer is:

$\dfrac{Q_i}{A t} = \dfrac{k_i (T_i - T_a)}{x}$

Equating the two heat flow rates:

$\dfrac{k_w (4 - 0)}{1.0 - x} = \dfrac{k_i (0 - (-10))}{x}$

Substituting the given values $k_w = 0.50 \text{ W m}^{-1}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $k_i = 1.7 \text{ W m}^{-1}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{0.50 \times 4}{1.0 - x} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10}{x}$

$\dfrac{2}{1.0 - x} = \dfrac{17}{x}$

$2x = 17(1.0 - x)$

$2x = 17 - 17x$

$19x = 17$

$x = \dfrac{17}{19} \approx 0.89 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three rods, each of length $20 \text{ cm}$ and cross-sectional area $1 \text{ cm}^2$, are joined together to form a triangle $ABC$. The thermal conductivities of these rods are $K_{AB} = 50 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, $K_{BC} = 200 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, and $K_{AC} = 400 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. The junctions $A$, $B$, and $C$ are maintained at temperatures of $40^\circ\text{C}$, $80^\circ\text{C}$, and $80^\circ\text{C}$ respectively. Determine the rate of heat flowing through the rods $AB$, $AC$, and $BC$.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through a rod is given by $H = \dfrac{KA\Delta T}{L}$.

Given:
Length of each rod, $L = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
Cross-sectional area, $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Temperatures at junctions: $T_A = 40^\circ\text{C}$, $T_B = 80^\circ\text{C}$, $T_C = 80^\circ\text{C}$

For rod $AB$:
$\Delta T_{AB} = T_B - T_A = 80^\circ\text{C} - 40^\circ\text{C} = 40^\circ\text{C}$
$H_{AB} = \dfrac{K_{AB} A \Delta T_{AB}}{L} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-4} \times 40}{0.2} = \dfrac{2000 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2} = 1 \text{ W}$

For rod $AC$:
$\Delta T_{AC} = T_C - T_A = 80^\circ\text{C} - 40^\circ\text{C} = 40^\circ\text{C}$
$H_{AC} = \dfrac{K_{AC} A \Delta T_{AC}}{L} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-4} \times 40}{0.2} = \dfrac{16000 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2} = 8 \text{ W}$

For rod $BC$:
$\Delta T_{BC} = T_B - T_C = 80^\circ\text{C} - 80^\circ\text{C} = 0^\circ\text{C}$
$H_{BC} = \dfrac{K_{BC} A \Delta T_{BC}}{L} = 0 \text{ W}$

The rates of heat flow through the rods $AB$, $AC$, and $BC$ are $1 \text{ W}$, $8 \text{ W}$, and $0$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A semicircular rod is connected at its ends to a straight rod made of the same material and having the same cross-sectional area. The straight rod acts as a diameter for the semicircular rod. The junctions are maintained at different temperatures. Determine the ratio of the heat transferred through a cross section of the semicircular rod to the heat transferred through a cross section of the straight rod in a given time interval.
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the semicircular rod be $R$.

The length of the straight rod is equal to the diameter of the semicircle, so $L_1 = 2R$.

The length of the semicircular rod is $L_2 = \pi R$.

Let the temperatures at the two junctions be $T_1$ and $T_2$. The rate of heat transfer through a rod of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and thermal conductivity $k$ is given by $H = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$.

The heat transferred through the semicircular rod in a given time interval $t$ is:

$Q_2 = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)t}{\pi R}$

The heat transferred through the straight rod in the same time interval $t$ is:

$Q_1 = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)t}{2R}$

The ratio of the heat transferred through the semicircular rod to that through the straight rod is:

$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{\dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)t}{\pi R}}{\dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)t}{2R}} = \dfrac{2R}{\pi R} = \dfrac{2}{\pi}$

The required ratio is $2 : \pi$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One end of a metal rod having a cross-sectional area of $1.0 \text{ cm}^2$ is being heated. At a certain instant, the temperature gradient is $5.0^\circ \text{C cm}^{-1}$ at cross section $A$ and $2.5^\circ \text{C cm}^{-1}$ at cross section $B$. Determine the rate at which the temperature is increasing in the section $AB$ of the rod. The heat capacity of the section $AB$ is $0.40 \text{ J }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$, and the thermal conductivity of the rod's material is $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$. Neglect any heat loss to the atmosphere.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow at cross-section $A$ is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$H_A = K A \left| \dfrac{dT}{dx} \right|_A$

Substituting the given values:

$H_A = 200 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5.0 \times 10^2) = 10 \text{ W}$

Similarly, the rate of heat flow at cross-section $B$ is:

$H_B = K A \left| \dfrac{dT}{dx} \right|_B$

$H_B = 200 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (2.5 \times 10^2) = 5 \text{ W}$

The net rate of heat accumulation in the section $AB$ is the difference between the heat entering at $A$ and leaving at $B$:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = H_A - H_B = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ W}$

The rate at which the temperature is increasing in section $AB$ is related to the net heat accumulation by:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{C} \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

Given the heat capacity $C = 0.40 \text{ J }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{5}{0.40} = 12.5^\circ\text{C s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Steam at $120^\circ C$ is continuously passed through a rubber tube that is $50 \text{ cm}$ long with inner and outer radii of $1.0 \text{ cm}$ and $1.2 \text{ cm}$, respectively. The room temperature is $30^\circ C$. Determine the rate of heat flow through the walls of the tube. The thermal conductivity of rubber is $0.15 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through a cylindrical wall is given by:

$H = \dfrac{2 \pi K L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$

Substituting the given values:

$K = 0.15 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$

$L = 0.5 \text{ m}$

$T_1 = 120^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_2 = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$

$r_1 = 1.0 \text{ cm}$, $r_2 = 1.2 \text{ cm}$

$H = \dfrac{2 \pi (0.15) (0.5) (120 - 30)}{\ln(1.2/1.0)}$

$H = \dfrac{0.15 \pi (90)}{\ln(1.2)}$

Using $\ln(1.2) \approx 0.1823$ and $\pi \approx 3.1416$:

$H = \dfrac{13.5 \times 3.1416}{0.1823}$

$H \approx 232.6 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest integer gives $233 \text{ J s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow tube possesses a length $l$, inner radius $R_1$, and outer radius $R_2$. The material has a thermal conductivity $K$. Determine the heat flowing through the walls of the tube if the flat ends are maintained at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ ($T_2 > T_1$).
questions.solution:
The heat flows longitudinally along the length of the tube because the temperature difference is maintained across its flat ends.

The cross-sectional area of the hollow tube through which heat flows is the area of the annular region, given by $A = \pi R_2^2 - \pi R_1^2 = \pi(R_2^2 - R_1^2)$.

The length of the tube is $l$ and the temperature difference is $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$.

According to the law of thermal conduction, the rate of heat flow is $H = \dfrac{KA\Delta T}{l}$.

Substituting the values, we get $H = \dfrac{K \pi (R_2^2 - R_1^2)(T_2 - T_1)}{l}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow tube possesses a length $l$, inner radius $R_1$, and outer radius $R_2$. The material has a thermal conductivity $K$. Determine the heat flowing through the walls of the tube if the inside of the tube is maintained at temperature $T_1$ and the outside is maintained at $T_2$.
questions.solution:
Consider an elemental cylindrical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$.

The surface area of this shell is $A = 2\pi r l$.

According to Fourier's law of heat conduction, the rate of heat flow $H$ is given by:

$H = -K A \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -K (2\pi r l) \dfrac{dT}{dr}$

Rearranging the terms for integration:

$\dfrac{dr}{r} = -\dfrac{2\pi K l}{H} dT$

Integrating both sides within the limits $r = R_1$ to $R_2$ and $T = T_1$ to $T_2$:

$\int_{R_1}^{R_2} \dfrac{dr}{r} = -\dfrac{2\pi K l}{H} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi K l}{H} (T_1 - T_2)$

Taking the magnitude of heat flow, we get:

$H = \dfrac{2\pi K l (T_2 - T_1)}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A central hole of radius $r_1$ is created in a uniform circular disc having thickness $d$ and radius $r_2$. The inner surface (a cylinder of length $d$ and radius $r_1$) is kept at a temperature $\theta_1$, while the outer surface (a cylinder of length $d$ and radius $r_2$) is kept at a temperature $\theta_2$ (with $\theta_1 > \theta_2$). Given that the thermal conductivity of the disc's material is $K$, determine the heat flowing per unit time through the disc.
questions.solution:
Consider a cylindrical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ within the disc.

The surface area of this shell through which heat flows radially is $A = 2\pi r d$.

The thermal resistance of this elemental shell is given by $dR = \dfrac{dr}{KA} = \dfrac{dr}{K(2\pi r d)}$.

The total thermal resistance of the disc is obtained by integrating from $r = r_1$ to $r = r_2$:

$R = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{2\pi K r d} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi K d} \ln\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$

The rate of heat flow is given by $H = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{R}$, where $\Delta \theta = \theta_1 - \theta_2$.

$H = \dfrac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{\dfrac{1}{2\pi K d} \ln\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)} = \dfrac{2\pi K d (\theta_1 - \theta_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper rod is joined to a steel rod as shown in the figure. Both rods possess equal length and identical cross sectional area. The copper rod's free end is maintained at $0^\circ C$ while the steel rod's free end is maintained at $100^\circ C$. Determine the temperature at the junction of the two rods. The thermal conductivity of copper is $390 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and that of steel is $46 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Let the temperature at the junction be $T$.

In steady state, the rate of heat flow through the copper rod is equal to the rate of heat flow through the steel rod.

$\dfrac{K_{Cu} A (T - 0)}{L} = \dfrac{K_{Steel} A (100 - T)}{L}$

Given that $K_{Cu} = 390 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and $K_{Steel} = 46 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$, and the rods have equal length $L$ and identical cross-sectional area $A$:

$390 (T - 0) = 46 (100 - T)$

$390 T = 4600 - 46 T$

$436 T = 4600$

$T = \dfrac{4600}{436} \approx 10.55^\circ C$

Rounding to one decimal place, we get $T = 10.6^\circ C$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A composite slab is formed by pasting together two plates of thicknesses $L_1$ and $L_2$ and thermal conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$. The slabs possess equal cross-sectional area. Determine the equivalent conductivity of the composite slab.
questions.solution:
For a composite slab connected in series, the equivalent thermal resistance is the sum of the individual thermal resistances.

$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$

Using the formula for thermal resistance $R = \dfrac{L}{KA}$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area:

$\dfrac{L_1 + L_2}{K_{eq} A} = \dfrac{L_1}{K_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{K_2 A}$

$\dfrac{L_1 + L_2}{K_{eq}} = \dfrac{L_1 K_2 + L_2 K_1}{K_1 K_2}$

$K_{eq} = \dfrac{K_1 K_2 (L_1 + L_2)}{L_1 K_2 + L_2 K_1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A liquid-nitrogen container is constructed from a $1$-cm thick styrofoam sheet with a thermal conductivity of $0.025 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$. Liquid nitrogen at $80 \text{ K}$ is stored inside. A total area of $0.80 \text{ m}^2$ is in contact with the liquid nitrogen. Given that the atmospheric temperature is $300 \text{ K}$, determine the rate of heat flow from the atmosphere into the liquid nitrogen.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through the styrofoam sheet is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(T_2 - T_1)}{d}$

Given values are:
Thermal conductivity, $K = 0.025 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
Area, $A = 0.80 \text{ m}^2$
Temperature difference, $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 300 \text{ K} - 80 \text{ K} = 220 \text{ K}$
Thickness, $d = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$

Substituting these values into the formula:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 0.80 \times 220}{0.01}$

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 220}{0.01}$

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{4.4}{0.01} = 440 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Forming three sides of a square, a steel frame ($K = 45 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$) has a total length of $60 \text{ cm}$ and a cross sectional area of $0.20 \text{ cm}^2$. The two free ends are maintained at $20^\circ C$ and $40^\circ C$. Determine the rate of heat flow through a cross section of the frame.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through a conducting rod is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$H = \dfrac{KA \Delta T}{L}$

Given:
Thermal conductivity, $K = 45 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
Cross-sectional area, $A = 0.20 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$
Total length of the frame, $L = 60 \text{ cm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
Temperature difference between the ends, $\Delta T = 40^\circ\text{C} - 20^\circ\text{C} = 20^\circ\text{C}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$H = \dfrac{45 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 20}{0.6}$

$H = \dfrac{1800 \times 10^{-5}}{0.6}$

$H = 3000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$

$H = 0.03 \text{ W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, an aluminium rod and a copper rod, each having a length of $1.0 \text{ m}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1 \text{ cm}^2$, are welded together. One end of the combination is maintained at $20^\circ \text{C}$ while the other end is kept at $60^\circ \text{C}$. Determine the amount of heat taken out per second from the hot end. The thermal conductivity of aluminium is $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$ and that of copper is $390 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ \text{C}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
From the given figure, the aluminium and copper rods are connected in parallel.

The total heat current (heat taken out per second) is the sum of the heat currents through each rod:
$H = H_{\text{Al}} + H_{\text{Cu}}$

Using the formula for heat conduction, $H = \dfrac{KA\Delta T}{L}$:
$H = \dfrac{K_{\text{Al}} A \Delta T}{L} + \dfrac{K_{\text{Cu}} A \Delta T}{L}$
$H = \dfrac{(K_{\text{Al}} + K_{\text{Cu}}) A \Delta T}{L}$

Given values are:
$K_{\text{Al}} = 200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$K_{\text{Cu}} = 390 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$\Delta T = 60^\circ\text{C} - 20^\circ\text{C} = 40^\circ\text{C}$
$L = 1.0 \text{ m}$

Substituting the values into the equation:
$H = \dfrac{(200 + 390) \times 10^{-4} \times 40}{1.0}$
$H = 590 \times 40 \times 10^{-4}$
$H = 23600 \times 10^{-4}$
$H = 2.36 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

Therefore, the amount of heat taken out per second is $2.36 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, an aluminium rod is joined to a copper rod. Both rods possess a length of $20 \text{ cm}$ and a cross-sectional area of $0.20 \text{ cm}^2$. The junction between them is kept at a constant temperature of $40^\circ C$, while their outer ends are maintained at $80^\circ C$. Determine the amount of heat extracted from the cold junction in one minute once the steady state has been reached. The thermal conductivities are given as $K_{Al} = 200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and $K_{Cu} = 400 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Length of each rod, $L = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
Cross-sectional area, $A = 0.20 \text{ cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$
Temperature of outer ends, $T_{hot} = 80^\circ C$
Temperature of junction, $T_{cold} = 40^\circ C$
Temperature difference across each rod, $\Delta T = 80^\circ C - 40^\circ C = 40^\circ C$

The rate of heat flow (heat current) through the aluminium rod is:
$H_{Al} = \dfrac{K_{Al} A \Delta T}{L} = \dfrac{200 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 40}{0.2} = 0.8 \text{ W}$

The rate of heat flow through the copper rod is:
$H_{Cu} = \dfrac{K_{Cu} A \Delta T}{L} = \dfrac{400 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 40}{0.2} = 1.6 \text{ W}$

The total rate at which heat reaches the cold junction is:
$H_{total} = H_{Al} + H_{Cu} = 0.8 + 1.6 = 2.4 \text{ W}$

To maintain the steady state, the heat extracted from the cold junction in one minute ($t = 60 \text{ s}$) is:
$Q = H_{total} \times t = 2.4 \times 60 = 144 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A room features a window equipped with a single $1.0 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m}$ glass of thickness $2 \text{ mm}$. Determine the rate of heat flow through the closed window if the temperature inside the room is $32^\circ C$ while the outside temperature is $40^\circ C$. The thermal conductivity of the window glass is $1.0 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through a material is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$H = \dfrac{K A \Delta T}{d}$

Given:
Area of the glass, $A = 1.0 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m} = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$
Thickness of the glass, $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
Temperature difference, $\Delta T = 40^\circ\text{C} - 32^\circ\text{C} = 8^\circ\text{C}$
Thermal conductivity, $K = 1.0 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$H = \dfrac{1.0 \times 2.0 \times 8}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$

$H = \dfrac{16}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$

$H = 8000 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the situation depicted in the figure. The frame is constructed from the same material and possesses a uniform cross-sectional area throughout. Determine the amount of heat flowing per second through a cross section of the bent part, given that the total heat extracted per second from the end at $100^\circ \text{C}$ is $130 \text{ J}$.
questions.solution:
The thermal circuit consists of a parallel combination of the straight middle part and the bent part, connected in series with the two end parts.

The length of the straight middle part is $L_1 = 60 \text{ cm}$.

The length of the bent part is $L_2 = 5 \text{ cm} + 60 \text{ cm} + 5 \text{ cm} = 70 \text{ cm}$.

Since the frame is made of the same material and has a uniform cross-sectional area, the thermal resistance $R$ of any part is proportional to its length $L$ ($R = \dfrac{L}{KA}$).

Let the thermal resistance of the straight part be $R_1 = 60k$ and that of the bent part be $R_2 = 70k$, where $k$ is a constant.

The total heat current flowing through the system is given as $I = 130 \text{ J/s}$.

In a parallel combination, the heat current divides inversely as the thermal resistance. The heat current $I_2$ flowing through the bent part is:

$I_2 = I \times \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$

Substituting the values:

$I_2 = 130 \times \dfrac{60k}{60k + 70k}$

$I_2 = 130 \times \dfrac{60}{130}$

$I_2 = 60 \text{ J/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A room features a window of dimensions $1.0 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m}$ equipped with two glasspanes. Each glasspane has a thickness of $1 \text{ mm}$ and they are separated by an air gap of $1 \text{ mm}$. Determine the rate of heat flow through the closed window if the temperature inside the room is $32^\circ C$ while the outside temperature is $40^\circ C$. The thermal conductivity of the window glass is $1.0 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$ and that of air is $0.025 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ C^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Area of the window, $A = 1.0 \times 2.0 = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$

Thickness of each glass pane, $d_g = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

Thickness of the air gap, $d_a = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

Temperature difference, $\Delta T = 40^\circ C - 32^\circ C = 8^\circ C$

The system consists of two glass panes and one air gap connected in series. The equivalent thermal resistance is given by:

$R_{eq} = R_{g1} + R_a + R_{g2} = \dfrac{d_g}{K_g A} + \dfrac{d_a}{K_a A} + \dfrac{d_g}{K_g A}$

$R_{eq} = \dfrac{2d_g}{K_g A} + \dfrac{d_a}{K_a A}$

Substituting the given values:

$R_{eq} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 2.0} + \dfrac{10^{-3}}{0.025 \times 2.0}$

$R_{eq} = 10^{-3} + \dfrac{10^{-3}}{0.05} = 10^{-3} + 20 \times 10^{-3} = 21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ C \text{ W}^{-1}$

The rate of heat flow is:

$H = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{8}{21 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{8000}{21} \approx 381 \text{ J s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rods with identical geometrical dimensions are placed between two heat baths maintained at $100^\circ C$ and $0^\circ C$, as shown in the figure. The temperature at their junction is $70^\circ C$. Determine the new junction temperature if the two rods are interchanged.
questions.solution:
Let $R_1$ and $R_2$ be the thermal resistances of the two rods.

In the initial arrangement, the heat current through both rods is the same. Therefore,

$\dfrac{100 - 70}{R_1} = \dfrac{70 - 0}{R_2}$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{30}{R_1} = \dfrac{70}{R_2}$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{3}{7}$

When the rods are interchanged, let the new junction temperature be $T$. The heat current remains the same through both rods in the new series combination:

$\dfrac{100 - T}{R_2} = \dfrac{T - 0}{R_1}$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{100 - T}{T} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$

Substituting $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{7}{3}$, we get:

$\dfrac{100 - T}{T} = \dfrac{7}{3}$

$\Rightarrow 300 - 3T = 7T$

$\Rightarrow 10T = 300$

$\Rightarrow T = 30^\circ C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The three rods depicted in the figure have identical geometrical dimensions. In arrangement (a), heat flows from the hot end at a rate of $40 \text{ W}$. The thermal conductivities of aluminium and copper are $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $400 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ respectively. Determine the rate of heat flow when the rods are joined as in arrangement (a).
questions.solution:
Let the thermal resistance of the aluminium rod be $R_{Al} = \dfrac{L}{K_{Al}A} = R$.

Since $K_{Cu} = 2K_{Al}$, the thermal resistance of the copper rod is $R_{Cu} = \dfrac{L}{K_{Cu}A} = \dfrac{R}{2}$.

For arrangement (a), the equivalent thermal resistance is:

$R_a = R_{Al} + R_{Cu} + R_{Al} = R + \dfrac{R}{2} + R = \dfrac{5R}{2}$

The rate of heat flow in arrangement (a) is:

$H_a = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_a} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{5R}{2}} = \dfrac{40}{R}$

Given $H_a = 40 \text{ W}$, we have $\dfrac{40}{R} = 40 \implies R = 1 \text{ }^\circ\text{C W}^{-1}$.

For arrangement (b), the equivalent thermal resistance is:

$R_b = R_{Al} + \dfrac{R_{Cu} R_{Al}}{R_{Cu} + R_{Al}} = R + \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{2} \cdot R}{\dfrac{R}{2} + R} = R + \dfrac{R}{3} = \dfrac{4R}{3}$

The rate of heat flow in arrangement (b) is:

$H_b = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_b} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{4R}{3}} = \dfrac{75}{R}$

Substituting $R = 1 \text{ }^\circ\text{C W}^{-1}$, we get $H_b = 75 \text{ W}$.

Answer: $75 \text{ W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cubical box with a volume of $216 \text{ cm}^3$ is constructed from wood of thickness $0.1 \text{ cm}$. Its interior is electrically heated using a $100 \text{ W}$ heater. During steady state, the temperature difference between the inside and outside surfaces is observed to be $5^\circ\text{C}$. Assuming all the consumed electrical energy is converted into heat, determine the thermal conductivity of the box's material.
questions.solution:
Volume of the cubical box is $V = 216 \text{ cm}^3$

Side length of the cubical box is $a = V^{1/3} = 6 \text{ cm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$

Total surface area of the cubical box is $A = 6a^2 = 6 \times (0.06)^2 = 0.0216 \text{ m}^2$

Thickness of the wood is $d = 0.1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

In steady state, the rate of heat flow equals the power of the heater, so $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = 100 \text{ W}$

Using the formula for thermal conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA \Delta T}{d}$

Substituting the given values:

$100 = \dfrac{K \times 0.0216 \times 5}{10^{-3}}$

$K = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0216 \times 5}$

$K = \dfrac{0.1}{0.108} \approx 0.926 \text{ W m}^{-1} ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Rounding to two decimal places, we get $0.93 \text{ W m}^{-1} ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The three rods depicted in the figure have identical geometrical dimensions. In arrangement (a), heat flows from the hot end at a rate of $40 \text{ W}$. The thermal conductivities of aluminium and copper are $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $400 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ respectively. Determine the rate of heat flow when the rods are joined as in arrangement (b).
questions.solution:
Let the thermal resistance of the aluminium rod be $R_{Al} = R$.

Since the thermal resistance is $R = \dfrac{L}{KA}$ and $K_{Cu} = 2K_{Al}$, the thermal resistance of the copper rod is $R_{Cu} = \dfrac{R}{2}$.

In arrangement (a), the three rods are in series. The equivalent thermal resistance is:

$R_a = R_{Al} + R_{Cu} + R_{Al} = R + \dfrac{R}{2} + R = \dfrac{5R}{2}$

The rate of heat flow is $H_a = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_a} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{5R}{2}} = \dfrac{40}{R}$.

Given $H_a = 40 \text{ W}$, we have $\dfrac{40}{R} = 40 \Rightarrow R = 1 \text{ K W}^{-1}$.

In arrangement (b), one aluminium rod is in series with a parallel combination of a copper rod and an aluminium rod. The equivalent resistance of the parallel part $R_p$ is:

$\dfrac{1}{R_p} = \dfrac{1}{R_{Cu}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{Al}} = \dfrac{2}{R} + \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{3}{R} \Rightarrow R_p = \dfrac{R}{3}$

The total equivalent resistance for arrangement (b) is:

$R_b = R_{Al} + R_p = R + \dfrac{R}{3} = \dfrac{4R}{3}$

The rate of heat flow in arrangement (b) is:

$H_b = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_b} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{4R}{3}} = \dfrac{75}{R}$

Substituting $R = 1 \text{ K W}^{-1}$, we get $H_b = 75 \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Seven rods $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$, $E$, $F$ and $G$ are connected as depicted in the figure. Every rod possesses an identical cross-sectional area $A$ and length $l$. The thermal conductivities of the respective rods are $K_A = K_C = K_0$, $K_B = K_D = 2K_0$, $K_E = 3K_0$, $K_F = 4K_0$ and $K_G = 5K_0$. The rod $E$ is maintained at a steady temperature $T_1$, while the rod $G$ is maintained at a steady temperature $T_2$ ($T_2 > T_1$). Determine the uniform temperature of the rod $F$.
questions.solution:
Let the thermal resistance of rod A be $R_A = \dfrac{l}{K_0 A} = R_0$.

The thermal resistances of the other relevant rods are:
$R_C = \dfrac{l}{K_0 A} = R_0$
$R_B = \dfrac{l}{2K_0 A} = \dfrac{R_0}{2}$
$R_D = \dfrac{l}{2K_0 A} = \dfrac{R_0}{2}$

Since rod E is maintained at a steady temperature $T_1$ and rod G is maintained at a steady temperature $T_2$, the junctions of A and C with E are at temperature $T_1$, and the junctions of B and D with G are at temperature $T_2$.

Let the temperature of the upper junction (between A, B, and F) be $T_x$ and the lower junction (between C, D, and F) be $T_y$.

Applying the principle of continuity of heat flow (Kirchhoff's junction law) at the upper junction:
$\dfrac{T_1 - T_x}{R_A} + \dfrac{T_2 - T_x}{R_B} + \dfrac{T_y - T_x}{R_F} = 0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T_1 - T_x}{R_0} + \dfrac{2(T_2 - T_x)}{R_0} + \dfrac{T_y - T_x}{R_F} = 0$ --- (1)

Similarly, at the lower junction:
$\dfrac{T_1 - T_y}{R_C} + \dfrac{T_2 - T_y}{R_D} + \dfrac{T_x - T_y}{R_F} = 0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T_1 - T_y}{R_0} + \dfrac{2(T_2 - T_y)}{R_0} + \dfrac{T_x - T_y}{R_F} = 0$ --- (2)

Due to the symmetry of the upper and lower branches ($R_A = R_C$ and $R_B = R_D$), the temperature of the upper and lower junctions must be equal, i.e., $T_x = T_y$.

Since $T_x = T_y$, no heat flows through rod F, and it possesses a uniform temperature $T_F = T_x = T_y$.

Substituting $T_y = T_x$ into equation (1):
$\dfrac{T_1 - T_x}{R_0} + \dfrac{2(T_2 - T_x)}{R_0} + 0 = 0$
$\Rightarrow T_1 - T_x + 2T_2 - 2T_x = 0$
$\Rightarrow 3T_x = T_1 + 2T_2$
$\Rightarrow T_x = \dfrac{T_1 + 2T_2}{3}$

Thus, the uniform temperature of rod F is $\dfrac{T_1 + 2T_2}{3}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{T_1 + 2T_2}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the rate of heat flow across a cross section of the rod illustrated in the figure ($\theta_2 > \theta_1$). The material of the rod has a thermal conductivity $K$.
questions.solution:
Consider an elemental disc of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the end with radius $r_1$.

The radius of this disc is given by $r = r_1 + \left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{L}\right)x$.

The thermal resistance of this elemental disc is $dR = \dfrac{dx}{K A} = \dfrac{dx}{K \pi r^2}$.

The total thermal resistance $R$ of the rod is the integral of $dR$ from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:

$R = \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{K \pi \left[ r_1 + \left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{L}\right)x \right]^2}$

Let $y = r_1 + \left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{L}\right)x$.

Differentiating with respect to $x$, we get $dy = \left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{L}\right)dx \Rightarrow dx = \dfrac{L}{r_2 - r_1} dy$.

The limits of integration change from $x=0 \to L$ to $y=r_1 \to r_2$.

$R = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{L}{K \pi (r_2 - r_1) y^2} dy$

$R = \dfrac{L}{K \pi (r_2 - r_1)} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{y} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2}$

$R = \dfrac{L}{K \pi (r_2 - r_1)} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right)$

$R = \dfrac{L}{K \pi (r_2 - r_1)} \left( \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{r_1 r_2} \right) = \dfrac{L}{K \pi r_1 r_2}$

The rate of heat flow is given by $H = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{R}$.

$H = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{\dfrac{L}{K \pi r_1 r_2}} = \dfrac{K \pi r_1 r_2 (\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{L}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The three rods depicted in the figure have identical geometrical dimensions. In arrangement (a), heat flows from the hot end at a rate of $40 \text{ W}$. The thermal conductivities of aluminium and copper are $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $400 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ respectively. Determine the rate of heat flow when the rods are joined as in arrangement (c).
questions.solution:
Let the length and cross-sectional area of each rod be $L$ and $A$ respectively.

The thermal resistance of a rod is given by $R = \dfrac{L}{KA}$.

Let the thermal resistance of the aluminium rod be $R_{Al} = R$. Since $K_{Cu} = 2K_{Al}$, the thermal resistance of the copper rod is $R_{Cu} = \dfrac{R}{2}$.

In arrangement (a), the three rods are connected in series. The equivalent thermal resistance is:

$R_{a} = R_{Al} + R_{Cu} + R_{Al} = R + \dfrac{R}{2} + R = \dfrac{5R}{2}$

The rate of heat flow in arrangement (a) is:

$H_a = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_a} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{5R}{2}} = \dfrac{40}{R}$

Given $H_a = 40 \text{ W}$, we have $\dfrac{40}{R} = 40 \Rightarrow R = 1 \text{ }^\circ\text{C W}^{-1}$.

In arrangement (c), the three rods are connected in parallel. The equivalent thermal resistance $R_c$ is given by:

$\dfrac{1}{R_c} = \dfrac{1}{R_{Al}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{Cu}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{Al}} = \dfrac{1}{R} + \dfrac{2}{R} + \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{4}{R}$

$\Rightarrow R_c = \dfrac{R}{4}$

The rate of heat flow in arrangement (c) is:

$H_c = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_c} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{R}{4}} = \dfrac{400}{R}$

Substituting $R = 1$, we get $H_c = 400 \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod with negligible heat capacity has a length of $20 \text{ cm}$, a cross-sectional area of $1.0 \text{ cm}^2$, and a thermal conductivity of $200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. One end's temperature is maintained at $0^\circ\text{C}$, while the temperature of the opposite end is slowly and linearly varied from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$ over a period of $10$ minutes. Assuming no heat is lost from the sides, determine the total heat transmitted through the rod during these $10$ minutes.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat transfer through the rod is given by Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA}{L} \Delta T$

Since the temperature of one end is maintained at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and the other end varies linearly from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$ over $10 \text{ minutes}$ ($600 \text{ s}$), the temperature difference $\Delta T$ as a function of time $t$ is $\Delta T(t) = \dfrac{60}{600} t = 0.1 t$.

The total heat transmitted $Q$ over the time interval $t = 0$ to $t = 600 \text{ s}$ is:

$Q = \int_{0}^{600} \dfrac{KA}{L} \Delta T(t) dt$

Substituting the given values $K = 200 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, $A = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, and $L = 0.2 \text{ m}$:

$\dfrac{KA}{L} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2} = 0.1 \text{ W }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Therefore, the integral becomes:

$Q = \int_{0}^{600} 0.1 \times 0.1 t dt = 0.01 \int_{0}^{600} t dt$

$Q = 0.01 \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{600}$

$Q = 0.01 \times \dfrac{360000}{2} = 1800 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow metallic sphere of radius $20 \text{ cm}$ encloses a concentric metallic sphere of radius $5 \text{ cm}$. The region between the two spheres is filled with a nonmetallic material. The inner and outer spheres are maintained at temperatures of $50^\circ \text{C}$ and $10^\circ \text{C}$ respectively, and it is observed that $100 \text{ J}$ of heat travels from the inner sphere to the outer sphere per second. Determine the thermal conductivity of the material between the spheres.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat flow through a spherical shell of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$ is given by

$H = \dfrac{4 \pi K r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$

where $K$ is the thermal conductivity of the material.

Given:

$H = 100 \text{ W}$

$r_1 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

$r_2 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$

$T_1 = 50^\circ \text{C}$

$T_2 = 10^\circ \text{C}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$100 = \dfrac{4 \pi K (0.05)(0.20)(50 - 10)}{0.20 - 0.05}$

$100 = \dfrac{4 \pi K (0.01)(40)}{0.15}$

$100 = \dfrac{1.6 \pi K}{0.15}$

$K = \dfrac{100 \times 0.15}{1.6 \pi}$

$K = \dfrac{15}{1.6 \pi} = \dfrac{150}{16 \pi} = \dfrac{75}{8 \pi} \text{ W m}^{-1}\text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two adiabatic vessels, as shown in the figure, each contain a mass $m$ of water at different temperatures. A metal rod of length $L$, area of cross section $A$, and thermal conductivity $K$ has its ends inserted into the water. Determine the time required for the temperature difference between the vessels to drop to half of its initial value. The specific heat capacity of water is $s$. Assume the heat capacity of the rod and the container are negligible, and ignore any heat loss to the atmosphere.
questions.solution:
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the temperatures of the water in the two vessels at time $t$, with $T_1 > T_2$.

The rate of heat flow through the metal rod from the hotter vessel to the colder vessel is given by:
$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$

The heat lost by the first vessel causes its temperature to decrease, while the heat gained by the second vessel causes its temperature to increase. The rates of change of their temperatures are:
$\dfrac{dT_1}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{ms} \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$
$\dfrac{dT_2}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{ms} \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

Let the temperature difference be $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$. The rate of change of this temperature difference is:
$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} = \dfrac{dT_1}{dt} - \dfrac{dT_2}{dt} = -\dfrac{2}{ms} \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

Substituting the expression for $\dfrac{dQ}{dt}$:
$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} = -\dfrac{2KA}{msL} (T_1 - T_2) = -\dfrac{2KA}{msL} \Delta T$

Separating the variables and integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = t$, where the temperature difference drops from its initial value $\Delta T_0$ to $\dfrac{\Delta T_0}{2}$:
$\int_{\Delta T_0}^{\Delta T_0/2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\int_0^t \dfrac{2KA}{msL} dt$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_0/2}{\Delta T_0}\right) = -\dfrac{2KA}{msL} t$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{2KA}{msL} t$

$-\ln 2 = -\dfrac{2KA}{msL} t$

$t = \dfrac{Lms}{2KA} \ln 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bodies having masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ along with specific heat capacities $s_1$ and $s_2$ are joined by a rod of length $l$, cross-sectional area $A$, thermal conductivity $K$, and negligible heat capacity. The entire system is thermally insulated. At time $t = 0$, the temperatures of the first and second bodies are $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively ($T_2 > T_1$). Determine the temperature difference between the two bodies at time $t$.
options: $(T_2 - T_1) \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \right)$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{KA(m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}{l m_1 m_2 s_1 s_2}$
$(T_2 - T_1) e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{KA m_1 m_2 s_1 s_2}{l (m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}$
$(T_2 - T_1) e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{KA(m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}{l m_1 m_2 s_1 s_2}$
$(T_2 - T_1) e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{KA}{l (m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}$
questions.solution:
Let the temperatures of the two bodies at time $t$ be $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ respectively.

The rate of heat flow from the second body to the first body is given by:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{l}$

The heat gained by the first body and lost by the second body changes their temperatures. The rates of change of temperatures are:

$\dfrac{d\theta_1}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{m_1 s_1} \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{KA(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{l m_1 s_1}$

$\dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{m_2 s_2} \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = -\dfrac{KA(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{l m_2 s_2}$

The rate of change of the temperature difference $\Delta \theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ is:

$\dfrac{d(\Delta \theta)}{dt} = \dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} - \dfrac{d\theta_1}{dt} = -\dfrac{KA}{l} (\theta_2 - \theta_1) \left( \dfrac{1}{m_1 s_1} + \dfrac{1}{m_2 s_2} \right)$

$\dfrac{d(\Delta \theta)}{dt} = -\dfrac{KA(m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}{l m_1 m_2 s_1 s_2} \Delta \theta$

Let $\lambda = \dfrac{KA(m_1 s_1 + m_2 s_2)}{l m_1 m_2 s_1 s_2}$. The equation becomes:

$\dfrac{d(\Delta \theta)}{dt} = -\lambda \Delta \theta$

Integrating both sides from $t = 0$ to $t$, where the initial temperature difference is $T_2 - T_1$:

$\int_{T_2 - T_1}^{\Delta \theta} \dfrac{d(\Delta \theta)}{\Delta \theta} = -\int_0^t \lambda dt$

$\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{T_2 - T_1} \right) = -\lambda t$

$\Delta \theta = (T_2 - T_1) e^{-\lambda t}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose that the total surface area of a human body is $1.6 \text{ m}^2$ and it emits radiation like an ideal radiator. Determine the amount of energy radiated per second by the body if the body temperature is $37^\circ \text{C}$. The Stefan constant $\sigma$ is taken as $6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$.
questions.solution:
Given, surface area $A = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$

Temperature $T = 37^\circ \text{C} = 37 + 273 = 310 \text{ K}$

Stefan constant $\sigma = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$

Since the body acts as an ideal radiator, emissivity $e = 1$.

The amount of energy radiated per second is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$P = \sigma e A T^4$

Substituting the given values:

$P = (6.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times 1 \times 1.6 \times (310)^4$

$P = 9.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 9235210000$

$P = 9.6 \times 92.3521$

$P = 886.58 \text{ J/s}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $887 \text{ J/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the amount of heat radiated per second by a body having a surface area of $12 \text{ cm}^2$, which is maintained in thermal equilibrium inside a room at a temperature of $20^\circ C$. The surface emissivity is $0.80$ and $\sigma = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$.
questions.solution:
The temperature of the body in thermal equilibrium with the room is $T = 20^\circ \text{C} = 293 \text{ K}$.

The surface area of the body is $A = 12 \text{ cm}^2 = 12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.

According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the amount of heat radiated per second by the body is given by:

$P = e \sigma A T^4$

Substituting the given values:

$P = 0.80 \times (6.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (12 \times 10^{-4}) \times (293)^4$

$P = 57.6 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.37 \times 10^9$

$P \approx 0.4245 \text{ W}$

Rounding off, the heat radiated per second is $0.42 \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid aluminium sphere and a solid copper sphere, the latter having twice the radius, are heated to identical temperatures and subsequently allowed to cool in identical surrounding temperatures. Assuming that the emissivity of both spheres is equal, determine the ratio of the rate of heat loss from the aluminium sphere to the rate of heat loss from the copper sphere. The specific heat capacity of aluminium is $900 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and that of copper is $390 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. The density of copper is $3.4$ times the density of aluminium.
questions.solution:
The rate of heat loss from a body is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = e \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)$

Since both spheres have the same emissivity $e$, are at identical temperatures $T$, and are in identical surrounding temperatures $T_0$, the rate of heat loss depends only on their surface areas $A$.

The surface area of a sphere is $A = 4 \pi R^2$, which implies $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} \propto R^2$.

Given that the copper sphere has twice the radius of the aluminium sphere, $R_{Cu} = 2R_{Al}$.

The ratio of the rate of heat loss from the aluminium sphere to that of the copper sphere is:

$\dfrac{(dQ/dt)_{Al}}{(dQ/dt)_{Cu}} = \dfrac{R_{Al}^2}{R_{Cu}^2} = \dfrac{R_{Al}^2}{(2R_{Al})^2} = \dfrac{1}{4}$

The specific heat capacity and density are extraneous information, as they are only required if the rate of cooling $\left(\dfrac{dT}{dt}\right)$ is asked.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid aluminium sphere and a solid copper sphere, the latter having twice the radius, are heated to identical temperatures and subsequently allowed to cool in identical surrounding temperatures. Assuming that the emissivity of both spheres is equal, determine the ratio of the rate of fall of temperature of the aluminium sphere to the rate of fall of temperature of the copper sphere. The specific heat capacity of aluminium is $900 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and that of copper is $390 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. The density of copper is $3.4$ times the density of aluminium.
questions.solution:
The rate of fall of temperature of a body is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{e \sigma A (T^4 - T_s^4)}{mc}$

For a solid sphere, the mass is $m = \rho V = \rho \left(\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3\right)$ and the surface area is $A = 4 \pi R^2$. Substituting these into the equation gives:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{e \sigma (4 \pi R^2) (T^4 - T_s^4)}{\rho \left(\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3\right) c} = \dfrac{3 e \sigma (T^4 - T_s^4)}{\rho c R}$

Since the temperatures, surrounding temperatures, and emissivities are identical for both spheres, the rate of fall of temperature $r$ is inversely proportional to the product of density, specific heat capacity, and radius:

$r \propto \dfrac{1}{\rho c R}$

The ratio of the rate of fall of temperature of the aluminium sphere to that of the copper sphere is:

$\dfrac{r_{Al}}{r_{Cu}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Cu} c_{Cu} R_{Cu}}{\rho_{Al} c_{Al} R_{Al}}$

Given values:
$R_{Cu} = 2 R_{Al}$
$\rho_{Cu} = 3.4 \rho_{Al}$
$c_{Al} = 900 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
$c_{Cu} = 390 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$

Substituting these values into the ratio:

$\dfrac{r_{Al}}{r_{Cu}} = \dfrac{3.4 \rho_{Al} \times 390 \times 2 R_{Al}}{\rho_{Al} \times 900 \times R_{Al}}$

$\dfrac{r_{Al}}{r_{Cu}} = \dfrac{3.4 \times 390 \times 2}{900}$

$\dfrac{r_{Al}}{r_{Cu}} = \dfrac{2652}{900} \approx 2.946$

Rounding to one decimal place, the ratio is approximately $2.9 : 1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ W}$ bulb has a tungsten filament with a total length of $1.0 \text{ m}$ and a radius of $4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$. The emissivity of the filament is $0.8$, and $\sigma = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$. Calculate the temperature of the filament when the bulb is operating at its correct wattage.
questions.solution:
The power radiated by the filament is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$P = e \sigma A T^4$

The surface area of the cylindrical filament is:

$A = 2 \pi r L$

Substituting the given values:

$A = 2 \pi \times (4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1.0 = 8 \pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$

Substituting $A$ into the power equation:

$100 = 0.8 \times (6.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (8 \pi \times 10^{-5}) \times T^4$

$100 = 38.4 \pi \times 10^{-13} \times T^4$

$T^4 = \dfrac{100}{38.4 \pi \times 10^{-13}}$

$T^4 = \dfrac{10^{15}}{120.64} \approx 8.29 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$

Taking the fourth root:

$T \approx (8.29)^{1/4} \times 10^3 \text{ K}$

Since $1.7^4 = 8.3521$, we get:

$T \approx 1.7 \times 10^3 \text{ K} = 1700 \text{ K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suspended inside a closed box maintained at $57^\circ \text{C}$, a spherical ball with a surface area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ absorbs all incident radiation. Determine the amount of radiation falling on the ball per second. Use a Stefan constant of $6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$.
questions.solution:
The temperature of the enclosure is $T = 57^\circ \text{C} = 57 + 273 = 330 \text{ K}$.

The surface area of the ball is $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.

According to Prevost's theory of heat exchange, the radiation incident on a body placed inside an enclosure depends only on the temperature of the enclosure. The amount of radiation falling on the ball per second is given by:

$E = \sigma A T^4$

Substituting the given values:

$E = (6.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (20 \times 10^{-4}) \times (330)^4$

$E = 120 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.186 \times 10^{10}$

$E \approx 1.42 \text{ J/s}$

The amount of radiation falling on the ball per second is approximately $1.4 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suspended inside a closed box maintained at $57^\circ \text{C}$, a spherical ball with a surface area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ absorbs all incident radiation. Determine the net rate of heat flow to or from the ball at an instant when its temperature is $200^\circ \text{C}$. Use a Stefan constant of $6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$.
questions.solution:
The temperature of the ball is $T = 200^{\circ}\text{C} = 473 \text{ K}$.

The temperature of the surroundings is $T_0 = 57^{\circ}\text{C} = 330 \text{ K}$.

The surface area of the ball is $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.

Since the ball absorbs all incident radiation, it behaves as a perfectly black body, giving an emissivity $e = 1$.

According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the net rate of heat flow is given by:

$P = \sigma e A (T^4 - T_0^4)$

Substituting the given values:

$P = (6.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times 1 \times (20 \times 10^{-4}) \times (473^4 - 330^4)$

$P = 12 \times 10^{-11} \times (5.005 \times 10^{10} - 1.186 \times 10^{10})$

$P = 12 \times 10^{-11} \times (3.819 \times 10^{10})$

$P \approx 4.58 \text{ W}$

Since $T > T_0$, the net heat flow is from the ball.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suspended in an evacuated chamber kept at $300 \text{ K}$, a spherical tungsten piece of radius $1.0 \text{ cm}$ is maintained at a temperature of $1000 \text{ K}$ via electrical heating. Determine the rate at which electrical energy must be supplied. The emissivity of tungsten is $0.30$ and the Stefan constant $\sigma$ is $6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$.
questions.solution:
The rate of electrical energy supplied must equal the net rate of heat loss by radiation to maintain a constant temperature.

According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the net rate of heat loss is given by:

$P = e \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)$

Given:

Emissivity, $e = 0.30$

Stefan's constant, $\sigma = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$

Radius, $r = 1.0 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

Surface area of the sphere, $A = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (10^{-2})^2 = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Temperature of the sphere, $T = 1000 \text{ K}$

Temperature of the surroundings, $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$P = 0.30 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \times (1000^4 - 300^4)$

$P = 1.8 \times 10^{-8} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \times (10^{12} - 81 \times 10^8)$

$P = 7.2\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{12} (1 - 0.0081)$

$P = 7.2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.9919$

$P \approx 22.4 \text{ W}$

The closest value among the given options is $22 \text{ W}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cubical block of mass $1.0 \text{ kg}$ and edge $5.0 \text{ cm}$ is heated to $227^\circ \text{C}$. It is placed in an evacuated chamber maintained at $27^\circ \text{C}$. Assuming the block emits radiation as a blackbody, determine the rate at which the block's temperature will decrease. The specific heat capacity of the block's material is $400 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
The surface area of the cubical block is $A = 6a^2$.

Substituting $a = 0.05 \text{ m}$, we get $A = 6 \times (0.05)^2 = 0.015 \text{ m}^2$.

According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the net rate of heat loss is:

$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)$

The rate of decrease of temperature is given by:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{mc} \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)}{mc}$

Given values are:

$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$

$T = 227^{\circ} \text{C} = 500 \text{ K}$

$T_0 = 27^{\circ} \text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$

$m = 1.0 \text{ kg}$

$c = 400 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

Substituting these values into the equation:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.015 \times (500^4 - 300^4)}{1.0 \times 400}$

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.015 \times (625 \times 10^8 - 81 \times 10^8)}{400}$

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{5.67 \times 0.015 \times 544}{400}$

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{46.2672}{400} \approx 0.1156 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C s}^{-1}$

Rounding to two significant figures, the rate of temperature decrease is $0.12 \text{ }^{\circ}\text{C s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the setup depicted in the figure. Assume that the thermal conductivity of the bent part of the frame is $780 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, while for the straight part it is $390 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. Determine the ratio of the rate of heat flow through the bent part to the rate of heat flow through the straight part.
questions.solution:
Let the temperature difference across the parallel section be $\Delta T$.

The rate of heat flow through a conductor is given by $H = \dfrac{KA\Delta T}{L}$, where $K$ is the thermal conductivity, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $L$ is the length.

For the bent part:
Thermal conductivity, $K_b = 780 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
Length of the bent part, $L_b = 5 \text{ cm} + 60 \text{ cm} + 5 \text{ cm} = 70 \text{ cm}$
Rate of heat flow, $H_b = \dfrac{K_b A \Delta T}{L_b}$

For the straight part in parallel:
Thermal conductivity, $K_s = 390 \text{ J s}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
Length of the straight part, $L_s = 60 \text{ cm}$
Rate of heat flow, $H_s = \dfrac{K_s A \Delta T}{L_s}$

The ratio of the rate of heat flow through the bent part to that through the straight part is:
$\dfrac{H_b}{H_s} = \dfrac{K_b}{K_s} \times \dfrac{L_s}{L_b}$

Substituting the given values:
$\dfrac{H_b}{H_s} = \left(\dfrac{780}{390}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{60}{70}\right)$

$\dfrac{H_b}{H_s} = 2 \times \dfrac{6}{7} = \dfrac{12}{7}$

Thus, the required ratio is $12 : 7$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper sphere is suspended within an evacuated chamber kept at $300 \text{ K}$. The sphere is kept at a constant temperature of $500 \text{ K}$ by heating it electrically, which requires $210 \text{ W}$ of electric power. Once the surface of the copper sphere is completely blackened, $700 \text{ W}$ is required to maintain the sphere at the same temperature. Determine the emissivity of copper.
questions.solution:
According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the net rate of heat loss by radiation from a body is given by $P = e \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)$.

For the copper sphere, the power required to maintain its temperature is:

$P_1 = e \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4) = 210 \text{ W}$

When the sphere is completely blackened, it behaves as a black body with an emissivity of $1$. The power required is:

$P_2 = (1) \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4) = 700 \text{ W}$

Dividing the equation for the copper sphere by the equation for the blackened sphere, we get:

$e = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{210}{700} = 0.3$

Thus, the emissivity of copper is $0.3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical ball $A$ having a surface area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ is positioned at the centre of a hollow spherical shell $B$ of area $80 \text{ cm}^2$. The surface of $A$ and the inner surface of $B$ both emit as blackbodies. $A$ and $B$ are both maintained at a temperature of $300 \text{ K}$. Determine the radiation energy emitted per second by the ball $A$.
questions.solution:
According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the radiation energy emitted per second by a blackbody is given by:

$E = \sigma A T^4$

For the spherical ball $A$, the given values are:
$A = 20 \text{ cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$T = 300 \text{ K}$
$\sigma \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$

Substituting these values into the formula:

$E = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (20 \times 10^{-4}) \times (300)^4$

$E = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 20 \times 10^{-4} \times 81 \times 10^8$

$E = 5.67 \times 1620 \times 10^{-4}$

$E \approx 0.918 \text{ J/s}$

The closest option provided is $0.94 \text{ J}$. (Note: Taking the Stefan-Boltzmann constant as $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$ yields exactly $0.9396 \approx 0.94 \text{ J/s}$).

Answer: $0.94 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical ball $A$ having a surface area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ is positioned at the centre of a hollow spherical shell $B$ of area $80 \text{ cm}^2$. The surface of $A$ and the inner surface of $B$ both emit as blackbodies. $A$ and $B$ are both maintained at a temperature of $300 \text{ K}$. Calculate the radiation energy emitted per second by the inner surface of $B$.
questions.solution:
According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the total radiation energy emitted per second by a blackbody surface is given by:

$E = \sigma A T^4$

For the inner surface of the hollow spherical shell $B$:
Area, $A_B = 80 \text{ cm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Temperature, $T = 300 \text{ K}$
Stefan-Boltzmann constant, $\sigma \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$E_B = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 80 \times 10^{-4} \times (300)^4$

$E_B = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 80 \times 10^{-4} \times 81 \times 10^8$

$E_B = 5.67 \times 80 \times 81 \times 10^{-4}$

$E_B = 3.674 \text{ J/s}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get approximately $3.8 \text{ J}$.
(Note: If $\sigma$ is approximated as $5.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$ as is sometimes done to match specific options, $E_B = 3.76 \text{ J/s} \approx 3.8 \text{ J/s}$).

Answer: $3.8 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical ball $A$ having a surface area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$ is positioned at the centre of a hollow spherical shell $B$ of area $80 \text{ cm}^2$. The surface of $A$ and the inner surface of $B$ both emit as blackbodies. $A$ and $B$ are both maintained at a temperature of $300 \text{ K}$. How much of the energy emitted per second by the inner surface of $B$ falls back onto this surface itself?
questions.solution:
Let $F_{AB}$ be the fraction of energy emitted by sphere $A$ that falls on sphere $B$. Since $A$ is completely enclosed by $B$, all radiation emitted by $A$ falls on $B$, giving $F_{AB} = 1$.

Using the reciprocity theorem for thermal radiation:

$A_A F_{AB} = A_B F_{BA}$

$F_{BA} = \dfrac{A_A}{A_B} F_{AB} = \dfrac{20}{80} \times 1 = 0.25$

The fraction of energy emitted by $B$ that falls back onto its own inner surface is:

$F_{BB} = 1 - F_{BA} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$

The total energy emitted per second by the inner surface of $B$ (acting as a blackbody) is given by Stefan-Boltzmann's law:

$E_B = \sigma A_B T^4$

The energy falling back onto $B$ per second is:

$E_{BB} = F_{BB} E_B = 0.75 \sigma A_B T^4$

Substituting the given values ($\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $A_B = 80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$):

$E_{BB} = 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 80 \times 10^{-4} \times (300)^4$

$E_{BB} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 60 \times 10^{-4} \times 81 \times 10^8$

$E_{BB} = 5.67 \times 60 \times 81 \times 10^{-4}$

$E_{BB} = 5.67 \times 0.486 \approx 2.756 \text{ J/s}$

Rounding to one decimal place, the energy falling back onto the surface is $2.8 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical rod with a length of $50 \text{ cm}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1 \text{ cm}^2$ is positioned between a large ice chamber at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and an evacuated chamber kept at $27^\circ\text{C}$, as shown in the figure. Very small portions of the rod extend into the chambers, while the remainder is thermally insulated from the surroundings. The cross section entering the evacuated chamber is blackened, allowing it to completely absorb any incident radiation. Once steady state is achieved, the temperature of this blackened end is $17^\circ\text{C}$. Given the Stefan constant $\sigma = 6 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W m}^{-2} \text{ K}^{-4}$, determine the thermal conductivity of the rod's material.
questions.solution:
In steady state, the net rate of heat absorbed by the blackened end via radiation equals the rate of heat conducted through the rod.

The net rate of heat absorbed by radiation is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:
$P_{\text{rad}} = \sigma A (T_0^4 - T_1^4)$
where $T_0 = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$ is the temperature of the evacuated chamber, and $T_1 = 17^\circ\text{C} = 290 \text{ K}$ is the temperature of the blackened end.

The rate of heat conduction through the rod is:
$P_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$
where $T_2 = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273 \text{ K}$ is the temperature of the ice chamber, and $L = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$.

Equating $P_{\text{rad}}$ and $P_{\text{cond}}$:
$\sigma A (T_0^4 - T_1^4) = \dfrac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$

$\Rightarrow k = \dfrac{\sigma L (T_0^4 - T_1^4)}{T_1 - T_2}$

Substituting the given values:
$T_0^4 - T_1^4 = (300)^4 - (290)^4 = 81 \times 10^8 - 70.7281 \times 10^8 = 10.2719 \times 10^8 \text{ K}^4$

$k = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \times 10.2719 \times 10^8}{290 - 273}$

$k = \dfrac{3 \times 10.2719}{17} = \dfrac{30.8157}{17} \approx 1.8 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

Answer: $1.8 \text{ W m}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four identical rods $AB$, $CD$, $CF$ and $DE$ are connected as shown in the figure. For each rod, the length, cross-sectional area, and thermal conductivity are $l$, $A$, and $K$ respectively. The ends $A$, $E$, and $F$ are kept at temperatures $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_3$ respectively. Assuming no heat loss to the atmosphere, determine the temperature at $B$.
questions.solution:
Let the thermal resistance of a rod of length $l$ be $R = \dfrac{l}{KA}$.

The resistances of the given rods are:
$R_{AB} = R$
$R_{CF} = R$
$R_{DE} = R$

Since CD is a single rod of length $l$ and B is its midpoint, the lengths of sections CB and BD are $l/2$. Therefore, their thermal resistances are:
$R_{CB} = \dfrac{R}{2}$
$R_{BD} = \dfrac{R}{2}$

Let the temperatures at junctions B, C, and D be $T_B$, $T_C$, and $T_D$ respectively. Applying the principle of continuity of heat flow (Kirchhoff's junction law) at junction C:
$\dfrac{T_3 - T_C}{R} + \dfrac{T_B - T_C}{R/2} = 0$
$\Rightarrow T_3 - T_C + 2(T_B - T_C) = 0$
$\Rightarrow 3T_C = T_3 + 2T_B$
$\Rightarrow T_C = \dfrac{T_3 + 2T_B}{3}$

Applying the junction law at D:
